provisions.title: IOSCO Board and Committees Structure
provisions.text: IOSCO's standard-setting work is conducted through its Board and eight policy committees: C1 (Issuer Accounting, Audit and Disclosure); C2 (Regulation of Secondary Markets); C3 (Regulation of Market Intermediaries); C4 (Enforcement and the Exchange of Information); C5 (Investment Management); C6 (Credit Rating Agencies); C7 (Commodity Derivatives); and C8 (Retail Investors). The IOSCO Board has 34 members representing the world's largest securities markets. IOSCO's three objectives are: protecting investors; ensuring fair, efficient, and transparent markets; and reducing systemic risk.

provisions.title: IOSCO Code of Conduct for CRAs
provisions.text: The IOSCO Code of Conduct Fundamentals for Credit Rating Agencies addresses: quality and integrity of the rating process (analytical rigour, rating methodologies, internal controls); independence and conflicts of interest (separation of rating and commercial activities, disclosure of conflicts); transparency and timeliness of ratings disclosure (publication policies, rating symbols, historical performance data); and confidential information handling. CRAs should establish a compliance function to monitor adherence to the code and should publish an annual transparency report.

provisions.title: CRA Methodology and Model Governance
provisions.text: CRAs should establish governance processes for the development, validation, and modification of rating methodologies. Rating methodologies should be: rigorous, systematic, and where possible, result in ratings that can be subjected to objective validation; applied consistently across asset classes and geographies; subject to regular review and update; and transparently disclosed. Methodology changes should include a consultation period and an assessment of the impact on existing ratings. CRAs should maintain model risk management frameworks.

provisions.title: IOSCO Principles for Financial Benchmarks
provisions.text: The IOSCO Principles for Financial Benchmarks establish standards for benchmark administrators covering: governance (oversight function, conflicts of interest management); quality of the benchmark (design, data sufficiency, hierarchy of inputs); quality of the methodology (robustness, transparency, periodic review); and accountability (complaints handling, audit trail, cooperation with regulatory authorities). Administrators should have a code of conduct for submitters and should publish a benchmark statement describing the methodology, data sources, and potential limitations.

provisions.title: IOSCO Transition from IBORs
provisions.text: IOSCO has supported the FSB-led transition from interbank offered rates to alternative reference rates. IOSCO's recommendations for benchmark transition include: adherence to the IOSCO Principles for Financial Benchmarks by alternative rate administrators; adequate fallback provisions in contracts referencing IBORs; transparent communication about transition timelines; and supervisory monitoring of transition progress. IOSCO has also published guidance on good practices for benchmark administrators seeking to cease publication of a benchmark.

provisions.title: IOSCO Guidance on ESG Ratings and Data Products
provisions.text: IOSCO has published recommendations on ESG ratings and data products providers. The recommendations cover: transparency (disclosure of methodologies, data sources, and material changes); governance (conflicts of interest management, quality assurance processes); systems and controls (robust operational infrastructure, data validation processes); and management of conflicts of interest (separation of ESG rating activities from consulting and advisory services). IOSCO recommends that ESG ratings providers adhere to a voluntary code of conduct pending further regulatory development.

provisions.title: IOSCO Guidance on Sustainability-Related Practices in Asset Management
provisions.text: IOSCO has published recommendations on sustainability-related practices in the asset management industry. Key areas include: product-level disclosures (sustainability characteristics, investment strategy, key metrics); entity-level disclosures (governance arrangements, stewardship practices, climate-related risk management); regulatory approaches to sustainability-related fund labels and classifications; addressing greenwashing risks; and supervisory expectations for sustainability-related claims. Asset managers should have appropriate systems and processes to substantiate sustainability-related claims.

provisions.title: IOSCO Guidance on Social Media and Retail Investor Protection
provisions.text: IOSCO has assessed the impact of social media on retail investor behaviour and market integrity. Key concerns include: the amplification of market manipulation through social media (pump and dump schemes, coordinated trading); the use of influencers and finfluencers to promote financial products without adequate disclosure; gamification features in trading apps that may encourage excessive risk-taking; and the spread of misleading information through social media platforms. IOSCO recommends enhanced market surveillance to detect social media-driven manipulation.

provisions.title: IOSCO Suitability Requirements
provisions.text: IOSCO requires that market intermediaries assess suitability when making personal recommendations to retail clients. Suitability assessment should consider: the client's investment objectives; the client's financial situation (income, assets, liabilities); the client's risk tolerance; the client's knowledge and experience with relevant investment products; and the client's investment time horizon. Intermediaries should maintain records of suitability assessments and should have policies for managing conflicts of interest in the recommendation process.

provisions.title: IOSCO Standards for Exchanges and Trading Platforms
provisions.text: IOSCO has published standards for the regulation of exchanges and trading platforms covering: fair and orderly markets (pre-trade and post-trade transparency, market surveillance); access requirements (objective, transparent, and non-discriminatory criteria); trading rules (order types, matching algorithms, market-making obligations); market integrity (prevention of market manipulation, insider trading controls); technology governance (system capacity, resilience, disaster recovery); and regulatory reporting (transaction reporting, position reporting, reference data).

provisions.title: IOSCO Standards for Dark Pools and Crossing Networks
provisions.text: IOSCO has published principles for the regulation of dark pools and crossing networks. Key requirements include: pre-trade transparency (appropriate thresholds for waiver from pre-trade transparency); post-trade transparency (timely publication of transactions executed in dark venues); fair access (objective and transparent criteria for participation); priority of displayed orders (measures to protect lit market quality); monitoring and oversight (regulatory review of dark pool volumes and market quality impacts); and information barriers (preventing information leakage from dark pool order flow).

provisions.title: Shortening the Settlement Cycle
provisions.text: IOSCO has assessed the implications of shortening the securities settlement cycle. The move from T+2 to T+1 settlement (implemented in the US and Canada in May 2024, and India in 2023) aims to reduce counterparty credit risk, reduce margin requirements, and increase market efficiency. Key implementation considerations include: operational readiness of market participants; cross-border implications (time zone differences, FX settlement alignment); securities lending recall timelines; ETF creation/redemption processes; and corporate actions processing.

provisions.title: IOSCO Standards for Market Abuse Enforcement
provisions.text: IOSCO has published standards for the effective enforcement of securities laws against market abuse. Key elements include: adequate legal authority to investigate and prosecute market manipulation, insider trading, and fraud; access to trading records, communications, and bank records; cross-border enforcement cooperation through the MMoU and EMMoU; deterrent sanctions (administrative, civil, and criminal); whistleblower programmes (protection, incentives); and the ability to obtain temporary restraining orders and asset freezes to preserve assets during investigations.
